narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which evaluates the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 55.5% chance that you would not pass—crossing the threshold we use to make admission decisions. This prediction draws from your academic record, including your undergraduate GPA of 2.80, LSAT score of 34, Year 1 and Year 3 decile rankings both at 3, and your middle family income bracket, along with your plan to attend full-time. Among students who passed the bar, the average LSAT is 37.27, undergraduate GPAs average 3.24, and Year 1 and Year 3 deciles are typically around 6 and 5.9, respectively—your metrics fell short in ways that shaped the model's view. The three most influential factors in this decision were your LSAT score, Year 3 decile, and Year 1 decile, ranked by their impact on pushing the prediction one way or the other. Your LSAT score had the strongest pull toward predicting success, as it suggested a solid foundation despite being below the typical passer’s level, helping to offset some concerns. In contrast, your Year 3 decile ranking was the top factor driving the prediction toward failure, indicating performance that lagged behind what the model sees in successful bar takers. Your Year 1 decile played a similar role, reinforcing that early academic standing as a key reason the model leaned toward risk. Being in the middle income bracket and planning full-time study aligned more closely with passers—middle income makes up about 35% of them, and 93% go full-time—but these didn't outweigh the academic signals. Overall, your profile compared to accepted students shows room for growth in grades and test scores, where typical admits cluster higher and demonstrate steadier upward trajectories. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your undergraduate record through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and gathering strong letters highlighting your potential. Many students in similar positions improve these areas and succeed on reapplication—your full-time commitment and background position you well for that effort. With targeted steps, you can shift the odds in your favor next time.
